metadata.title: The ATS-Friendly Resume (why the robot rejects you)
metadata.playlist: Ship It — Build Your Brand's Live Presence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 8 brief — Building Your Live Website, Resumes & LinkedIn Header

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 6. Before a human reads your resume, a parser does — and it throws away anything it can't read. ATS-friendly is not boring. It is strategic. Single column, keywords from the actual job post, and brand that lives in color and font — not in graphics. That is the whole model.

props.segment: The ATS-Friendly Resume (why the robot rejects you)
props.command: Single column · keywords from the job post · brand lives in color and font, not in graphics.
output: Single column — the ATS reads left-to-right; columns mangle the order.
Keywords from the actual job post — not generic, not invented.
Brand in safe elements only: color on headers, font on name — zero graphics.

beats.narration_text: ATS stands for Applicant Tracking System. Every major company and most medium-sized companies run incoming resumes through one before a recruiter opens them. The ATS extracts text — your name, your skills, your job titles, your employment dates — and then scores that extracted text against keywords from the job description. If your score falls below the company's threshold, the resume is filtered out and a human never sees it. The ATS does not read images. It does not read tables. It does not read text that lives inside a column layout, a text box, or a decorative shape. It reads plain extractable text, in order, from top to bottom. Understanding that one constraint tells you everything about how to structure a resume that gets through.

props.artifactTitle: ATS — what it is and what it does
props.artifactHeading: ATS — what it is and what it does
artifactLines: ATS = Applicant Tracking System. Runs before a recruiter opens your resume.
It extracts text — name, skills, job titles, dates — and scores against job-post keywords.
Below the threshold: filtered out. A human never sees it.
What the ATS cannot read: images, tables, columns, text boxes, decorative shapes.
What it reads: plain extractable text, top to bottom, in order.
props.sparkLine: One constraint explains the entire format.

beats.narration_text: Five things break ATS parsing, and all five are extremely common in resume templates people download from Notion, Canva, or design marketplaces. First, two-column layout. The ATS reads columns left-to-right across the full page, not column by column — so your skills from the right column get interleaved with your job titles from the left column, producing garbled text that scores near zero. Second, tables. Most parsers skip table content entirely or extract it as noise. Third, text inside images or graphics — the ATS sees a blank space where your name or skills should be. Fourth, decorative section headers in a font the parser can't extract — section breaks disappear and the text runs together. Fifth, skills listed inside a progress bar, a star rating, a bubble chart, or any non-text format. The bar means nothing to the parser. The skill keyword is invisible. Your beautiful two-column Notion template is, from the ATS perspective, nearly empty.

artifactLines: 1. Two-column layout — ATS reads left-to-right across the page; skills and titles get interleaved into noise.
2. Tables — most parsers skip table content or extract it as garbage.
3. Text inside images or graphics — the parser sees a blank where your name or skills should be.
4. Decorative section headers in an unextractable font — section breaks disappear, text runs together.
5. Skills in progress bars, star ratings, or bubbles — the keyword is invisible to the parser.
A beautiful two-column Notion template is, from the ATS's view, nearly empty.
props.sparkLine: The parser reads text. Everything else is silence.

beats.act: the single-column rule and strategic keywords
beats.narration_text: The format answer is a single column. Every line of text reads in order from top to bottom, no ambiguity about left or right, no table structure, no text boxes. The content answer is keywords from the actual job post — not keywords you think sound good, not generic skill words, not terms from a different company's listing for the same role. The ATS scores your resume against the specific words in the specific job description you're applying to. That means you extract the keywords before you write or prompt for the resume. Paste this prompt with the job description to get a keyword list you can use directly.

props.segment: The single-column rule and strategic keywords
props.command: Here is a job description: [PASTE JOB DESCRIPTION]. Extract the 10 most important keywords — the specific skills, tools, credentials, and action verbs the role requires. Format them as a comma-separated list I can use directly in my resume.
output: 10 keywords from the actual post — not generic, not guessed.
Skills, tools, credentials, and action verbs the role names explicitly.
Use these in your Experience bullets and Skills section verbatim.

beats.act: where subtle brand lives without breaking parsing
beats.narration_text: Brand does not disappear in an ATS-friendly resume. It moves to elements the parser ignores — which are the same elements the human reviewer actually sees when they open the document after it clears the ATS. Here is what is safe. An accent color applied to section headers via direct text formatting — not a shape, not a highlight layer, not an image. A brand font used for your name and headers, provided it is a standard system font or a commonly installed Google Font. Exotic or custom fonts are a risk because some parsers convert text to plain characters first. A plain-text version of your name at the top, in your brand font, as the first line of the document. A colored horizontal rule line between sections, implemented as a styled paragraph border in Word or as an HR element in HTML. What is not safe: icons before skill names, progress bars encoding proficiency levels, any image element, any shape or graphic. The human sees the color. The parser reads the text. Those are compatible goals.

props.artifactTitle: Safe brand elements in an ATS resume
props.artifactHeading: Safe brand elements in an ATS resume
artifactLines: Safe: accent color on section headers — applied as direct text formatting, not a shape or image.
Safe: brand font on your name and headers — system or common Google Font; exotic fonts risk misparse.
Safe: plain-text name at top in your brand font — first line, no graphic wrapper.
Safe: colored horizontal rule lines — styled paragraph border in Word, or HR in HTML.
Not safe: icons, progress bars, star ratings, any image element, any graphic shape.
The human sees the color. The parser reads the text. Both goals are compatible.
props.sparkLine: Brand in format, not in graphics.

beats.act: export PDF and Word and why
beats.narration_text: Export both formats. PDF preserves your visual formatting for the human reviewer who opens it after the ATS clears it — your brand font, your accent color, and your section layout all survive a PDF export. Word, or in some cases plain text, is what many ATS systems actually parse under the hood. Some systems strip the PDF and convert it to plain text before scoring. The rule is this: if the job application portal accepts a Word file, include it alongside the PDF. If the portal only accepts one file, submit PDF. Never submit only a Word file if PDF is an option, because Word formatting can shift across machines. And if you cannot be sure which format the ATS handles better — submit both when the portal allows it. The extra step takes under a minute and removes one source of unnecessary rejection.

artifactLines: PDF — preserves visual formatting for the human reviewer; brand font and color survive export.
Word — what many ATS systems actually parse; some strip PDFs and convert before scoring.
Rule: if the portal accepts Word, include it alongside PDF.
If portal is PDF-only, submit PDF.
Never Word-only if PDF is an option — Word formatting shifts across machines.
When in doubt: submit both. The extra step takes under a minute.
props.sparkLine: Two formats, one minute, one fewer rejection reason.

beats.narration_text: Four traps that show up in nearly every first ATS resume attempt. Trap one: the two-column template. It is the most common ATS-breaker in the wild because it is also the most common resume template available for download. If your current resume has a left sidebar, it is two-column. Start over with a single-column layout. Trap two: skills listed in a graphic — a star rating for proficiency, a bubble chart, a progress bar. The ATS reads zero of those. The keyword is invisible. List skills as plain text in a Skills section. Trap three: keyword stuffing. Exact matches matter for ATS scoring, but a wall of keywords with no context hurts readability for the human reviewer who opens it after it clears the filter. Put keywords inside actual experience bullets — the tool did this task, not just this skill word. Trap four: submitting only a PDF when Word is also accepted. PDF is not always the safest parse. Give the system both formats when the portal allows it.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Two-column template: the most common ATS-breaker; if there's a left sidebar, start over single-column.
Trap 2 — Skills in a graphic: star ratings, bubbles, and progress bars are invisible to the parser; list skills as plain text.
Trap 3 — Keyword stuffing: exact matches help, but a wall of keywords hurts the human reviewer; put keywords inside experience bullets.
Trap 4 — PDF-only when Word is accepted: PDF is not always the safest parse; submit both formats when the portal allows.
props.sparkLine: The parser and the reviewer are two different audiences.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Episode 6. Single column — no exceptions. Keywords come from the actual job post you are applying to, extracted before you write or prompt for the resume. Brand lives in safe elements: accent color on section headers as direct text formatting, your brand font on the name and headers — no icons, no graphics, no progress bars. Export both PDF and Word when the portal accepts both. Keyword stuffing is not a strategy — keywords belong inside experience bullets, not in a wall at the bottom. Never put skills inside a graphic format. The ATS reads text. If it is not text, it does not exist.

artifactLines: Single column — no exceptions; columns mangle parse order.
Keywords from the actual job post — extracted before writing, used inside experience bullets.
Brand in safe elements only: color on headers, brand font on name — no graphics.
Export both PDF and Word when the portal accepts both formats.
Never put skills inside a graphic format — the ATS reads text only.

beats.narration_text: Your Turn. Start with the job description, not your resume. Paste it into the keyword extraction prompt and get your list of ten keywords before you open any document. Then paste this resume prompt with your actual experience, your target role and company type, your extracted keyword list, and your brand specifics — your font name and your accent hex code — as plain text color only, no graphics. The output is clean Markdown with clear section headers: Summary, Experience, Skills, Education. You will convert that Markdown to Word and to PDF. Fact-check every generated line before you submit. The AI cannot verify your job titles, your dates, or your results — you can. Do not submit a resume you have not read line by line.

props.segment: The ATS-Friendly Resume (why the robot rejects you)
props.command: I need an ATS-optimized single-column resume. My background: [PASTE YOUR EXPERIENCE]. Target role: [ROLE_TITLE] at [COMPANY TYPE]. Keywords to include: [YOUR EXTRACTED KEYWORD LIST]. Brand: name in [FONT], section headers in [ACCENT_HEX] (plain text color only — no graphics). Output in clean Markdown with clear section headers: Summary, Experience, Skills, Education. I will convert to Word and PDF.
props.runningText: drafting ATS resume — fact-check everything before using…
output: Step 1: Extract keywords from the job post first — then paste this prompt.
Step 2: Fact-check every line — titles, dates, results are yours to verify.
Step 3: Convert Markdown to Word + PDF. Submit both when the portal allows.

beats.narration_text: The ATS-Friendly Resume — why the robot rejects you. Liam, in for Bear.
